shared.title: BACKGROUND TO THE CHACO WAR AND RESOLUTION OF THE CONFLICT
paragraphs:
Since the 19th century, the definition of the border boundaries of Bolivia and Paraguay in the area of the Chaco Boreal had been a matter of dispute. In response to this, international commissions of a neutral nature made different proposals for distribution that never came to fruition. 

 In the absence of agreements at the beginning of the 20th century, Bolivia and Paraguay began the occupation of the territory through the establishment of military forts. Paraguay also promoted the settlement of Mennonite colonies in the area from the 1920s. 

 After the end of the War, after three years of negotiations, on July 21, 1938, the two parties finally reached an agreement on the boundaries of the Chaco through the Treaty of Peace, Friendship and Borders. Paraguay obtained 80% of the territory in dispute, while Bolivia managed to secure its oil sovereignty and a port on the Paraguay River (Puerto Busch), therefore gaining access to the Atlantic Ocean. 

 In April 2009, seventy-four years after the end of the conflict, the presidents of Bolivia and Paraguay agreed on the definitive delineation of the boundaries between the two countries by signing the Final Report regarding this.
